narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 51.7% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is just over the threshold we use to flag higher-risk applicants. This prediction is based on your academic profile, including your second-decile ranking in Year 1 and Year 3 grades—much lower than the typical student's fifth- or sixth-decile standing—along with your LSAT score of 23.5, undergraduate GPA of 3.1, upper-middle family income bracket, Asian racial background, female gender, and plan to attend full-time. Compared to other students, your LSAT stands out as notably below the average of 36.7, while your GPA is only slightly under the usual 3.21, and your family income aligns reasonably with the dataset's midpoint. The model's decision was most heavily swayed by your Year 3 decile rank, which strongly nudged the prediction toward expecting a bar failure because it signals weaker performance relative to peers. Right behind that was your LSAT score, which also pushed in the same concerning direction due to its low value compared to the norm. Your Year 1 decile rank came next in influence, again contributing toward the failure prediction for similar reasons of being in the lower ranks. Following those were your family income, undergraduate GPA, and racial background, each adding a bit more weight toward the riskier outcome, though less forcefully. Your full-time status and gender had the smallest roles, still tilting slightly toward failure but not as dominantly. Overall, these factors combined to tip the scales just past 50% risk, unlike stronger profiles that pass with flying colors. To boost your future chances, focus on building stronger study habits and academic performance, as your lower decile ranks suggest room for improvement through targeted prep that could mimic higher-ranked students' success. Consider LSAT retakes or supplemental courses if possible, since that score was a key drag, and emphasize any upward trends in your record. With dedication to these areas, you could shift your profile toward the passing predictions we see in typical approved applicants. Keep pushing—you have solid foundations like your GPA and full-time commitment to build on.
